Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a standard source array with a direct HTTPS download from the project's official GitHub releases page: `https://github.com/pbek/QOwnNotes/releases/download/v${pkgver}/qownnotes-${pkgver}.tar.xz`. This is a trusted and expected source for the package. The `sha256sums` array provides a fixed checksum for the archive, which will be verified during source retrieval, ensuring integrity.

There is no command substitution, `pkgver()` function override, or top-level code execution outside of standard variable assignments. The only potentially dynamic behavior would be in the implicit `pkgver` sourcing (used by `makepkg --printsrcinfo`), but since `pkgver` is explicitly set to a fixed version (`26.6.7`) in the global scope, no runtime version fetching occurs. Thus, during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`, only safe operations are performed: downloading a fixed tarball and parsing static metadata.

The `prepare()`, `build()`, and `package()` functions are not executed due to the `--noprepare` and `--nobuild` flags, so their contents are out of scope for this narrow gate. No network requests, file operations, or code executions during the sourcing phase pose a risk.
</details>
<summary>
Safe source retrieval and static metadata only
</summary>
</security_assessment>

The `prepare()` function adds a simple build definition for "AUR" in a header file, which is benign and commonly done in AUR packages to indicate origin. The `build()` and `package()` functions use standard `cmake` commands consistent with the upstream build instructions. No network access or file operations occur during packaging that would compromise system security. All dependencies are legitimate and required for building and running the application.
</details>
<summary>

LLM auditresponse for PBE.QOwnNotes.desktop:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a standard `.desktop` entry for the QOwnNotes application, used by desktop environments to integrate applications into menus and launchers. It defines basic metadata such as name, comment, icon, categories, and actions. The `Exec` fields reference the `QOwnNotes` binary with specific command-line arguments to trigger internal application actions (e.g., creating a new note or showing the todo list), all of which are expected behaviors for this application.

There are no network operations, file manipulations, shell commands, or script executions present in this file. The content is declarative, not executable code, and adheres to the freedesktop.org desktop entry specification. All referenced actions and execution commands are confined to the application's own functionality and pose no risk of code injection, data exfiltration, or unauthorized system access.

None of the fields contain encoded or obfuscated content, suspicious URIs, or commands that reach outside the application's scope. The use of multiple translations and custom actions is typical for desktop entries of feature-rich applications like QOwnNotes.
</details>
<summary>
Standard desktop entry, no security risks
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for CMakeLists.txt:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The CMakeLists.txt file is a standard build configuration script for the QOwnNotes application, using CMake to define compilation options, source files, dependencies, and installation rules. It includes typical components such as Qt (version 5 or 6), bundled libraries (e.g., Botan, hunspell, libgit2 support via option), and internal modules for spell-checking, markdown editing, and networking. All paths referenced are local to the project's source tree (e.g., `libraries/...`), and no external downloads, script executions, obfuscated code, or network calls are present in the file.

It conditionally enables functionality based on build options (e.g., LanguageTool, Harper, aspell, libgit2), all of which are legitimate features documented by the project. The use of `find_package`, `add_subdirectory`, and `target_link_libraries` follows standard CMake practices. Dependencies are either bundled or resolved through the system package manager via PkgConfig (e.g., libsecret-1 for password storage). Installation directives are standard and place files in appropriate system directories (`bin`, `share/icons`, `share/applications`).

There is no evidence of malicious behavior such as data exfiltration, remote code execution, or unauthorized system access. The file does not invoke shell commands, curl/wget, eval, or any form of dynamic code loading. It is consistent with upstream build practices for a Qt-based desktop note-taking application.
</details>
<summary>
Standard CMake build script, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for FindBotan3.cmake:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `FindBotan3.cmake` is a CMake script module designed to locate the system-installed Botan 3 cryptographic library using PkgConfig. It contains standard logic for cross-platform build systems: it disables support on Windows with MSVC or MinGW (a common limitation), checks if the target `Botan3::Botan3` already exists to avoid redundant work, and otherwise uses `pkg_check_modules` to find the library via PkgConfig.

All operations are confined to standard CMake and PkgConfig functionality, with no suspicious file operations, network calls, or execution of external untrusted code. The use of `add_library` with `ALIAS` is normal CMake practice for imported targets. The script does not modify system state, exfiltrate data, or execute any payloads.

This file aligns with conventional build system behavior in CMake projects and poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
Standard CMake script; no malicious behavior</summary>
</security_assessment>

[4/8] Reviewing src/qownnotes-26.6.7/cmake/FindBotan2.cmake, src/qownnotes-26.6.7/debian/qownnotes.install, src/qownnotes-26.6.7/icons/breeze-dark-qownnotes/copy-from-breeze-dark.sh, src/qownnotes-26.6.7/icons/download-icon.sh...

LLM auditresponse for FindBotan2.cmake:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided CMake script `FindBotan2.cmake` is a standard module for locating the Botan 2 cryptographic library using `PkgConfig`. It performs no network operations, file modifications, or system commands beyond typical build configuration. The script checks if the target `Botan2::Botan2` already exists to avoid redundant processing, and on Windows, it explicitly disables system Botan usage with a fatal error—likely because the project bundles or expects a different build method there.

It correctly uses `find_package(PkgConfig)` and `pkg_check_modules` to locate `libbotan-2` via pkg-config, a standard practice in CMake projects. The resulting imported target is aliased appropriately. There is no obfuscated code, suspicious downloads, or dangerous command execution. All behavior aligns with expected build-system logic for locating a system dependency.

While it does not pin a specific version of Botan2 beyond requiring `botan-2`, such flexibility is common in build scripts and not inherently unsafe. The use of `REQUIRED` ensures the build fails if the dependency is not found, enhancing transparency.
</details>
<summary>
Standard CMake script for Botan 2 detection; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[5/8] Reviewing src/qownnotes-26.6.7/debian/qownnotes.install, src/qownnotes-26.6.7/icons/breeze-dark-qownnotes/copy-from-breeze-dark.sh, src/qownnotes-26.6.7/icons/download-icon.sh...

LLM auditresponse for qownnotes.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file `qownnotes.install` is a standard install script used by Arch Linux packages to define actions taken during package installation, upgrade, or removal. It consists solely of a list of file mappings, indicating how files from the package should be installed into the system hierarchy.

Each line follows the format `<source> <destination>`, which is consistent with Arch package conventions for installing desktop files, icons, translation files, and binaries. The script installs application icons in appropriate icon sizes under `/usr/share/icons/hicolor`, places desktop entries in `/usr/share/applications`, Qt translations in `/usr/share/qt5/translations`, and the main binary into `/usr/bin`. There are no executable commands, shell snippets, network operations, file deletions, or environment modifications.

This file contains no code or scripts—only declarative file placement instructions. It does not execute anything, read sensitive data, or modify system state beyond copying pre-packaged files to standard directories. There is no obfuscation, suspicious behavior, or evidence of tampering. The operations are entirely consistent with normal, safe packaging practices for a desktop application.
</details>
<summary>
Standard file mappings, no executable code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for copy-from-breeze-dark.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `copy-from-breeze-dark.sh` is a simple Bash script that copies SVG icon files from a specified Breeze Dark icon directory to a local project directory (`../../breeze-dark-qownnotes/16x16/`). The source path uses a NixOS-style system path (`/run/current-system/sw/share/`) instead of the standard `/usr/share`, which suggests it is tailored for a NixOS environment. This is not inherently malicious but rather a configuration choice.

The operations performed are limited to `cd`, `find`, `basename`, and `cp` on local filesystem paths. It does not execute downloaded code, make network requests, exfiltrate data, or use obfuscated commands. The script processes only SVG files found in a trusted system directory and copies them to a predictable local target. While relying on system state (like icon paths) can affect reproducibility, this does not constitute a supply chain attack or backdoor.

The script is consistent with a helper utility used in packaging or theme development to synchronize icons from a system theme into a project. There is no evidence of malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Copies local icons; no malicious code
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[7/8] Reviewing src/qownnotes-26.6.7/icons/download-icon.sh...
+ Reviewed src/qownnotes-26.6.7/icons/breeze-dark-qownnotes/copy-from-breeze-dark.sh. Status: SAFE -- Copies local icons; no malicious code
LLM auditresponse for download-icon.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `download-icon.sh` is a utility for copying icon files from the Nix-provisioned Breeze icon theme (light and dark variants) into project-specific directories (`breeze-qownnotes` and `breeze-dark-qownnotes`) and updating corresponding Qt resource files (`.qrc`) using `xmlstarlet`. It runs in a pure `nix-shell` environment with explicitly declared dependencies (`xmlstarlet`, `kdePackages.breeze-icons`), ensuring reproducibility and isolation.

All operations are local and self-contained: it locates the Breeze icons via `XDG_DATA_DIRS`, copies SVG files to predictable paths within the project, and modifies `.qrc` resource files by adding new `<file>` entries with aliases. There are no network operations, no dynamic code evaluation, no obfuscation, and no system modifications outside the immediate project directory. The use of `nix-shell` and fixed dependencies aligns with standard practices for reproducible development environments.

The script performs no telemetry, data exfiltration, or execution of external code. It manipulates only expected project files (icons and resource manifests), consistent with its documented purpose. While it uses `chmod` to ensure file writability, this is benign within the context of local resource management. No supply-chain red flags or malicious behaviors are present.
</details>
<summary>
Safe local icon copying and resource updating
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for FindBotan3.cmake:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `FindBotan3.cmake` is a standard CMake find module for locating the Botan 3 library using pkg-config. It checks for MSVC/MinGW (aborting with an error on Windows), checks if the target already exists, then uses `pkg_check_modules` to locate the library via `botan-3` and creates an alias target `Botan3::Botan3`. There are no suspicious operations, no network requests beyond normal pkg-config usage, no obfuscated code, and no deviation from standard packaging or build helper practices. The file is consistent with the expected behavior of a CMake finder module for an upstream library.
</details>
<summary>Standard CMake find module, no malicious content.</summary>
</security_assessment>

This is a standard CMake build file for the QOwnNotes application. It configures the project with CMake options, finds required Qt components, and includes bundled libraries (Botan, Sonnet, QHotKey, QtKeychain, FakeVim, etc.) for legitimate purposes like spell checking, cryptography, keyboard shortcuts, and syntax highlighting. The file lists all source files, sets up compilation flags, links libraries, and handles installation. There are no network requests, no execution of external commands like `curl` or `wget`, no obfuscated code, and no attempts to exfiltrate data or modify system files outside the installation tree. The only external dependencies are system libraries (Qt, libgit2, Aspell) found via `find_package` and `pkg_check_modules`, which is standard practice. The use of `include(libraries/sonnet/src/plugins/hunspell/hunspell/CMakeLists.txt)` and similar `add_subdirectory` commands for bundled libraries is normal for applications that vendor dependencies. Nothing in this file deviates from expected build system behavior for a QT application.
</details>

LLM auditresponse for download-icon.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script `download-icon.sh` is a straightforward helper for copying icons from the system's breeze-icons package (expected to be available via Nix) into the local package directories and updating Qt resource files (`.qrc`). It performs no network operations, does not execute any untrusted code, and does not exfiltrate data. The only file operations are reading SVG icons from a trusted system location and writing them to local directories, plus editing XML resource files with `xmlstarlet` – standard packaging steps. There is no obfuscation, no dangerous commands, and no deviation from legitimate packaging tasks. Even though it runs in a Nix shell, that is merely the environment declared by the shebang; the script itself is safe.
</details>
<summary>Legitimate icon-copying helper; no security concerns.</summary>
</security_assessment>
